title: Five words a day
summary: A personal account from a São Paulo woman who decided to learn just five words of Japanese a day and discovered how slow learning beats fast forgetting.
body:
My name is Beatriz, and I live in São Paulo. Three years ago I wanted to learn Japanese. My grandmother was Japanese, but she died when I was small, and I never learned a single word from her. I felt I had lost something.

So I bought a big book. The book had forty lessons. On the first weekend I studied for six hours. I learned maybe a hundred words. I was very proud. But on Monday I went back to work, and I was tired, and I did not open the book. I did not open it for two weeks. When I finally looked again, I had forgotten almost everything. I closed the book and felt stupid.

Then a friend gave me different advice. She said, 'Do not study for six hours. Study five words a day. Only five. Even when you are tired. Five words is two minutes.'

Five words felt too small to matter. But I tried it. Every morning, with my coffee, I learned five words. On a bad day, I still did five. On a good day, I did not do more — I stopped at five, so I always wanted to come back.

Five words a day is one thousand eight hundred words a year. After three years, I can read children's books in Japanese. I can write a short letter. Last month I read my grandmother's old recipe card. I cried, because I finally understood her handwriting.

The six-hour weekend taught me almost nothing, because I never came back. The two-minute morning taught me a language, because I always came back. Slow and small, but every single day — that is the trick nobody believes until they try it.

quá nhỏ để có ý nghĩa
tôi dừng ở con số năm
tấm thẻ ghi công thức nấu ăn

annotations.meaningVi: chậm và nhỏ, nhưng mỗi ngày

grammarNotes.title: Past simple vs past perfect for 'before' a past moment
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 chủ yếu dùng quá khứ đơn cho việc đã xong: 'I bought a big book', 'I studied for six hours.' Khi muốn nói một việc xảy ra TRƯỚC một mốc quá khứ khác, ta dùng quá khứ hoàn thành 'had + V3': 'When I finally looked again, I had forgotten almost everything' (quên TRƯỚC khi nhìn lại). Cấu trúc: 'had + quá khứ phân từ' (forget → forgotten, lose → lost). Dùng khi cần làm rõ thứ tự thời gian trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 mostly uses the past simple for finished actions: 'I bought a big book', 'I studied for six hours.' To show one event happened BEFORE another past moment, use the past perfect 'had + past participle': 'When I finally looked again, I had forgotten almost everything' (the forgetting came BEFORE looking). Form: 'had + past participle' (forget → forgotten, lose → lost). Use it to make the order of past events clear.

translationVi:
Tên tôi là Beatriz, và tôi sống ở São Paulo. Ba năm trước tôi muốn học tiếng Nhật. Bà tôi là người Nhật, nhưng bà mất khi tôi còn nhỏ, và tôi chưa từng học được một từ nào từ bà. Tôi cảm thấy mình đã mất một thứ gì đó.

Vậy nên tôi mua một cuốn sách lớn. Cuốn sách có bốn mươi bài học. Cuối tuần đầu tiên tôi học sáu tiếng. Tôi học được khoảng một trăm từ. Tôi rất tự hào. Nhưng thứ Hai tôi đi làm lại, và tôi mệt, và tôi không mở sách. Tôi không mở nó suốt hai tuần. Khi cuối cùng tôi nhìn lại, tôi đã quên gần hết. Tôi gấp sách lại và thấy mình ngốc nghếch.

Rồi một người bạn cho tôi lời khuyên khác. Cô ấy nói: 'Đừng học sáu tiếng. Học năm từ mỗi ngày. Chỉ năm thôi. Ngay cả khi mệt. Năm từ là hai phút.'

Năm từ thấy quá nhỏ để có ý nghĩa. Nhưng tôi thử. Mỗi sáng, cùng ly cà phê, tôi học năm từ. Ngày tệ, tôi vẫn học năm. Ngày tốt, tôi không học thêm — tôi dừng ở con số năm, để luôn muốn quay lại.

Năm từ một ngày là một nghìn tám trăm từ một năm. Sau ba năm, tôi có thể đọc sách thiếu nhi bằng tiếng Nhật. Tôi có thể viết một lá thư ngắn. Tháng trước tôi đọc tấm thẻ công thức cũ của bà. Tôi đã khóc, vì cuối cùng tôi hiểu được nét chữ của bà.

Cuối tuần sáu tiếng dạy tôi gần như chẳng gì cả, vì tôi không bao giờ quay lại. Buổi sáng hai phút dạy tôi cả một ngôn ngữ, vì tôi luôn quay lại. Chậm và nhỏ, nhưng mỗi ngày — đó là mẹo không ai tin cho đến khi họ thử.

questions.prompt: Why did Beatriz want to learn Japanese?
options: Her grandmother was Japanese, and she felt she had lost something
questions.explanation: 'My grandmother was Japanese... I felt I had lost something.'

questions.prompt: What did she do on the first weekend?
options: Studied for six hours and learned about a hundred words
questions.explanation: 'On the first weekend I studied for six hours. I learned maybe a hundred words.'

options: She was tired and did not open the book for two weeks
questions.explanation: 'I did not open it for two weeks.'

questions.prompt: What did she find when she looked at the book again?
questions.explanation: 'When I finally looked again, I had forgotten almost everything.'

questions.prompt: What advice did her friend give?
options: Study five words a day, even when tired
questions.explanation: 'Study five words a day. Only five. Even when you are tired.'

questions.prompt: Why did she stop at five even on a good day? (inference)
options: So she always wanted to come back
Because five was the maximum allowed
Because the book had only five words
questions.explanation: 'I stopped at five, so I always wanted to come back.'

questions.prompt: How many words is five a day in a year?
questions.explanation: 'Five words a day is one thousand eight hundred words a year.'

questions.prompt: What can she do after three years?
options: Read children's books and write a short letter in Japanese
questions.explanation: 'I can read children's books in Japanese. I can write a short letter.'

questions.prompt: Why did she cry last month?
options: She finally understood her grandmother's handwriting on a recipe card
questions.explanation: 'I read my grandmother's old recipe card. I cried, because I finally understood her handwriting.'

questions.prompt: What is the 'trick' she describes? (vocab/main detail)
options: Slow and small, but every single day
Fast and big, once a month
questions.explanation: 'Slow and small, but every single day — that is the trick.'

options: A tiny daily amount you actually keep up beats a big effort you abandon — and it reconnected her to her grandmother.
questions.explanation: Consistency over intensity, plus the emotional payoff with the recipe card.

cloze.template:
My name is Beatriz, and I live in São Paulo. Three years ago I wanted to learn Japanese. My grandmother was Japanese, but she died when I was small, and I never learned a single {{b1}} from her. I felt I had lost something.

So I bought a big {{b2}}. On the first weekend I studied for six {{b3}}. I learned maybe a hundred words. But on Monday I went back to work, and I was {{b4}}, and I did not open the book for two weeks. When I finally looked again, I had {{b5}} almost everything.

Then a friend gave me different advice. She said, 'Study five words a {{b6}}. Only five. Even when you are tired.'

Five words felt too small to {{b7}}. But I tried it. On a good day, I did not do more — I stopped at five, so I always wanted to come {{b8}}.

Five words a day is one thousand eight hundred words a year. After three years, I can read children's books in Japanese. Last month I read my grandmother's old recipe card. I cried, because I finally understood her {{b9}}.

Slow and small, but every single {{b10}} — that is the trick nobody believes until they try it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Beatriz found that a small daily habit beat a big burst of effort, mostly because she kept coming back. Why do you think we are so attracted to the 'six-hour weekend' even though it often fails? What is something you could try learning five units of, every single day?
